speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is February 25th, 1997. The survivor's name is Renee Scott. Interviewer's name is Clifford Davids. This interview is taking place in Boston, Mass, United States of America, in the English language. Today's date is February 25th, 1997. Survivor's name is Renee Scott. My name is Clifford Davids. This interview is taking place in Boston, Mass, in the United States of America, in the English language.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the second reading, and correct reading of the slate. Today's date is February 25th, 1997. The rescue and aid-giver's name is Renee Scott. My name is Clifford Davids. This interview is taking place in Boston, Mass, United States of America, in the English language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. Can I have your name, please, and the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Renee Scott. You want me to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: R-E-N-E-E. Scott, S-C-O-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I have your name at birth, and the spelling, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Renee Scott. That's my maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Have you ever had any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had Barro, my husband's name. B-A-R-R-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever had any other names, other than Barro?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever had any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mickey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-C-K-E-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13th of February, 1906.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 91 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I have the city, and the spelling, and the country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Calais, C-A-L-A-I-S, Calais, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me the names of your parents and any siblings that you had? Can I have the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Albert George Scott. My mother was Jean Galchet. G-A-L-C-H-E-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had seven brothers and sisters. I had two that I did not know. Two babies that died before Yvonne was born. Then I had Yvonne. I had George. I had Yvonne, George, me, May, and Walter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Scott. Let's start out by you talking about your childhood in Calais.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't remember in Calais. I was too small. I came to America. I was 2 and 1/2 years old, I think, when I came to America with my parents, where my dad was director of lace mills, in Somerville, New Jersey first, where he was part owner of the lace mill, Redfern Lace Mills. And he stayed there-- we stayed there for several years. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I went to school when I was a child, French and English School, as a ba-- you know, little child. And in Somerville I went to school. Then we kept moving around as my dad was moving in other, you know, other cities for the lace mills. And his last lace mill was here in Pawtucket, Rhode Island, where he was director of the American Textile Company for about six or seven years, when the war broke out. And we went-- we lived in-- in Providence, in Edgewood, Providence, And from there-- they were closing the mill on account of the war. And we went to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're referring to the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Should I continue?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to New York where Dad opened up a-- his office for making-- he was a designer in lace. So he attempted to make out lace designs, thinking that would help him during the war. But it didn't help him. So he decided to go back, as soon as the war was finished. He went back by himself to Calais, where he found everything demolished, all the lace mills. My two grandfathers were owners of lace mills in Calais. Everything was demolished. So we stayed here in New York for a while, my mother with the four children-- Yvonne, George, Walter, May, and I-- five of us. And in 19-- well, I think it was 1917 when the war finished. I think we went back about-- in '18, a little after 1918, we went back to join my dad. And there we continued to try to find work. We couldn't find work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did like lots of teenagers, you know, a little job here, a little job there. But we lived at my grandmother's house. We had no home. And we stayed there for quite a while. And my-- my dad was very lucky to get a job with the American Graves Registration in Belgium. I'm sorry, with the-- what was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American Grave Registrations. So we all went to Belgium. We went to Antwerp. And there we-- we had an apartment in a big hotel where they set us up, the American Grave Registration. We stayed there for about a year. And there I was-- found work in a bank. [LAUGHS] I always dared try everything. I didn't know, but I tried. And I didn't like that very much. I had learned to dance in America as a child, and I wanted to be a ballerina. So my parents were not crazy about it, but I said, that's what I'd like to do. So in Antwerp I joined a dancing school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this happened in-- I don't know. I would say-- my daughter was born in '25. In the '20s, probably. '21, I don't know. I can't remember. I'm an old lady, you know. You ask me dates I don't know exactly. You know, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I joined the dancing school. And I was very lucky to get a job right away-- almost right away. Because they knew, they saw I had learned to dance. I could do it. And I had a job right away at the big theater called the Palladium in Antwerp. So I told my sister, May, I said, you're too young but you have to join. And come with me and dance too. So I took her to dancing school, and she loved it. And she was a wonderful dancer. She danced the whole Swan Lake on her bare toes. She was a wonderful first dancer. So then from there we joined opera dancing. And we danced at the opera in Antwerp for two seasons. And then from there my parents decided to move. Where did we go? We went to Nancy, the east of France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we all-- we were very happy there. We had nice apartments. And my daughter was then about three years old then. And we stayed there in  Nancy for about eight years. And that's where my sister and I danced at the opera there for five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me backtrack a little bit. You said you studied dance when you were in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Providence.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Providence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the school that you studied at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right at the opera. The school was right in the opera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what style of dance did you learn in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Opera. Ballet. Especially ballet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you were in Antwerp, you danced there for approximately two or three years until you moved to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there we did everything

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living at home at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living at home at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've always lived at home. Always. Except when we traveled, you know. But we always lived at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your life during those years in Antwerp. Were you very active socially?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no time, really. I mean, no, we were always-- we worked. After rehearsal we went home. We never went out at night-- very, very, very-- no, we never went out at night. We just went for our work and we went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me about what church they belonged to.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was a Methodist minister. And my mother was very religious. And my father too. My father was a Freemason. He was Scotch and English. And we always had a very good religious background, and we still do now. And my grandchildren and my great grandchildren are also very religious. Not fanatic, but they are religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to church regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I did. I don't-- no, now I don't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go. I'm a member right here down the street. And I'm in touch with them all the time, but I cannot always get around. I can't go alone anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you consider your-- did you consider your family to be very political?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My dad, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did you tour with the dance company in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tour?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Flanders. In Flanders, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you moved to France, you had-- you had been dancing for a number of years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven years old when I started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dancing professionally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you arrived in France, did you join a company-- a dance company immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a company. At the opera. Not company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join the opera immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your-- your experiences there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, opera season, it lasts-- it starts in November, finishes in April, and during the whole winter. Like here at the [? Wayne, ?] you know. But here they have companies that come. You have no opera here in Boston. Have you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You haven't got an opera theater here. I don't know. I'm asking you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know either. Well in-- in Europe, every city has an opera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the season from November until April, you were working regularly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And in between I always worked. As I told you, I took public relations in fairs, or anything I could get. You know, but-- that was-- as long as it was honorable, I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the opera, you danced in the opera between November and April. And what did you do during the summertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: Public relation, usually with fairs. I traveled with fairs, and different things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about that-- about that job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I traveled with the Paris fair. And traveled mostly in Switzerland, and Germany. Like the-- like the trade fairs-- that's what it was-- trade fairs. So I was secretary in the fairs. Took that-- like you want to come and present what you were going to present. I would take your name, your address, and all the information. That was my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have met many interesting people while you did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have met many--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I met lots of nice people, interesting. Some not. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How many languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I speak French, English, and Flemish. Dutch. Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me more about your family life while you were living in France during those years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could I tell you? I don't know. My family life was very plain because we weren't rich at the time. We all had to work. And it wasn't, you know-- we were very happy at home. And we were happy with what we had. But we weren't-- we could-- we didn't go on vacations and things like that. Our family life was very, very-- how would I say-- calm and, and quiet, at home mostly. We'd go out, and church and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father, your father grew to accept your job as a dancer for the opera?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't like it, but he accepted it. I forgot to tell you, my dad spoke-- I told you-- seven and eight languages fluently. He graduated from Oxford University, and from the-- the University of Leipzig, in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother spoke fluent English and French, and wrote them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember of antisemitism back in France, back in the '20s when you were living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't take care of that at all, not at all. Never involved. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only in '38 when, you know, it started to be very bad between Germany and, and England when Eden-- remember when Eden came over. It started to be bad. That's when they asked me if I wanted to work with the underground. And my dad opposed, so I didn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk more about the years leading up to 1938, and about what you were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- when you were living in France, did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel at all as a dancer?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a dancer, oh, yeah. Travelled all over. All over France, all over North Africa, all over everywhere in, in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this would have been during the summertime, in the off-season?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then after, when-- when we stopped the opera, then we joined a company. And then we traveled 15 months at a time without coming home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you dance with the opera for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many years. Oh, I don't know. Every year we would dance in the opera, you know, since we were dancing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin traveling abroad with the dance company?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Right away after the-- the opera season. Then we had contracts with the companies to travel. I couldn't tell you what date. I don't know what date it was. As soon as we could [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] travel by ourselves-- my sister and I, my parents signed-- had to sign for us. And we travelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you travelled throughout the 1930s, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was opera. Before the war, it was always opera. It's only after the war that we start-- not-- not this war, the first, you know, the other war. In between the two wars, we traveled all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between approximately 19-- 1917, 1918, and 19-- 1940, you were traveling all over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Until 19-- until '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: '39. Tell me a little bit about the traveling that you did, and some of your experiences traveling with the dance company.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very interesting to go in different countries and-- I-- I loved Switzerland and Germany. We met a lot of people. Especially Switzerland, I loved Switzerland. And the people were very, very nice with the companies there-- very good. And we had a lot of success. My sister and I had a, a team dance, the two of us like you saw there. And we enjoyed our life as-- as dancers very much. But we weren't the kind to go out after the shows and all that. We did our work. And we were too tired to-- to even, you know-- to say we're going to go out. Yeah, we'd go and have dinner after, because we never-- never ate before we went on the stage. So we'd eat after the show, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get many invitations to go out for dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had a lot of-- we didn't accept them all, but we had a lot, lot of invitations.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were exposed to many different cultures in your travels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We met all kinds of people, all kinds of nationalities, and all kinds of religions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your exposure to the different types of culture was a factor in your helping the Jews out during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I think what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your travels throughout North Africa and Europe helped you to understand other peoples?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think more you, more you meet people of different nationalities or culture, as you'd call it-- like in Belgium, you meet, you know, different people than you do in Switzerland. And you learn a lot. You learn a lot by, by meeting all these different kind of people. I know I did. I met-- I learned to do all kinds of things and to accept people much better. You accept people better when you know th-- you know, when you meet people and-- and know the way they live and their culture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you consider yourself a very tolerant person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think I am. [LAUGHS] I think I'm very patient. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were traveling in the 1930s and dancing, were you aware of what was going on in Germany with the Nazi party?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get that.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were traveling in the 1930s--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much, no. No. Only in '38 when it really, you know, started. No. Before that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about 1938 when you first became aware of what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- you had to be aware. It was, like, declared all at once. They were-- they were going to enter. Right. If they-- if we wanted or not, they were going to enter France. And that's when, I told you, that we decided not to stay in-- in the north of France. We went to Paris because my parents were afraid. But, my daughter was just a little young girl, 12 years old, and I was still young. And my parents didn't want us to be there, exposed, at the hands of the Germans if they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Paris where my sister, oldest sister, was living with her husband. And he was director, at that time, of the Guaranty Trust Company, the bank in New York. So they found us a place to live in Paris, and we stayed in Paris. We-- we stayed there all the time. Even when I was arrested, my parents still had the same apartment. So Yvonne had been living in Paris before you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever travel through Germany while you were dancing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must tell you, I wasn't very fond of, of-- that's a culture that I, I didn't care for too much. They're very harsh people. They're hard. They're not that friendly. Now maybe, but at that time they were not. No, I didn't care so much for Germany. I loved Switzerland, Belgium. Italy I liked very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experiences in Germany while you were dancing that-- that

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --helped you form that opinion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember at your performances, any soldiers coming up to you inviting you out for dinner afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany,?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any members of the Nazi party coming and watching you perform in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, were there any Nazis who watched you dance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I couldn't tell you that. That was after the war. I know they, they were mean. Even after the war they didn't like us. I don't say all the Germans. They're, you know-- I've met some nice German people too. I'm not like that. But I mean the Nazi crowd, they were terrible. And they still are. They will never change.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your life when you moved to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris, when you first arrived in Paris, what was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left the north of France?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paris was just Paris. I mean, there was nothing special in Paris. There's always-- Paris is always special. [LAUGHS] But I mean, I was living with my parents and there was nothing special. I always wanted to get some work, which I did. Pardon me. Almost right away I found work, dancing. And dancing at the-- I danced at the Moulin Rouge in Paris, too, which was very exciting. You know, with the [LAUGHS] ooh-la-la. [LAUGHTER] Now, that was nice. I liked it there. And then I danced at the opera. And I, I take-- I took the place with Solange Schwarz who was one of the big opera dancers. And we played in And my sister was first dancer, and I was the second dancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much would you get paid for a night dancing, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very poor. I couldn't tell. I don't remember. But very poor. 700 French francs a month. 750, I think it was. That was very poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father doing for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was looking for work. He didn't have any work. That was during the beginning of the war-- very hard. He was an interpreter when he-- you know, when he found different things to do as an interpreter, he would do it. He would do anything when he found it. Excuse me I have to-- may I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you first arrived in Paris after you left the north of France, you were working as a dancer. How long did you work as a dancer for?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- after '38, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Off and on when they needed me. You know, I was like fill-in. You know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there for about-- I worked like that for about, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '38, '39-- not very long. Because then I met Simone. And I worked with her at the Chamber of Commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I left the dancing for a while. The war was coming on. And I had started to work with her at the office, the Chamber of Commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Simone's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pheter. P-H-E-T-E-R. Pheter. It's a German name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how you first got the job at the Belgian Chamber of Commerce.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her at the-- at a trade fair. I told you that I had been secretary for a gentleman that was a big industrial in Belgium. And he had his office in Paris at the Chamber of Commerce. And he asked me if I would be his secretary in Paris, because it would avoid him from, you know, going back and forth. And I told you that we had decided to get married, but unfortunately he died when I came back from the camp. But anyway, I, I got in touch with Simone at the fair, because she-- the Belgian Chamber of Commerce represented at the fair. It's always-- there's always one, you know that. In trade fairs. So she told me, well, you're going to come with me, and we're going to work together there. And that's how I started to work with the underground with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the first time you met Simone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was introduced to her by a friend that was Director of the Remington-- what-- how do you call it-- type machine, in Paris. And we had known him a long, long time because he used to live in Nancy where we lived. And after the theater, we would always go, as I told you, to have supper or something to eat. And he would always be there with friends. Mostly they would talk business, or they'd play a game of cards before they went home. And I met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day in Paris he came to where we lived, and he rang our bell. And I was surprised to see him. And I said, [? John, ?] what are you doing here? And he said, what are you doing here? Where have you been all these years? He had a big-- a daughter the age of mine. So I-- he said, well I'm coming here to see some precision machines that they were making-- not for the war but-- they said not for the war, but it was. So anyway, we stayed in touch with him. So I told him, I said, oh, I said, I'm looking for work. I have no work right now here. He said, I'm going to introduce you to somebody. And he introduced me to Simone. That's how I met her. And what did you think of her the first time that you met her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I said, I'm glad to know you, [LAUGHS] the first time I met her. And then she took a liking to me, and me to her right away. We became very, very close friends right away. And then I worked with her at the Chamber of Commerce. We never talked underground-- never, never. We did-- I didn't know anything. I didn't know she did anything. Only when I told you that I wanted to go to Belgium to obtain-- to see a lawyer to get my divorce. My husband was in the Belgium Congo and I wanted to, to quit that relationship. My daughter had never met her father, because he left me before she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, she said you're OK, you're going to work here with me. So I stayed and worked with her. And then one day I told her I had to go to Brussels. She said, I'll get you a pass. We're going to finish the tape right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me? Rest?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to finish the tape right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: May I have a rest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
